1: "The LORD reigns (YHWH mālāk); let the earth rejoice! Let all the distant coastlands be glad!" — the enthronement proclamation (same as Ps 99:1; 93:1) opens with the same formula as Ps 96:10. The "distant coastlands (ʾiyyîm rabbîm)" — the islands and coastlands at the edges of the known world (Isa 41:1; 42:4, 10, 12; 51:5; 66:19; Jer 31:10) — represent the remotest Gentile peoples. The joy that is commanded is responsive to the fact of divine rule: the earth doesn't create the reason for joy, it responds to the reality that YHWH already reigns. In ANE royal ideology, the king's accession was the occasion for universal celebration; Psalm 97 applies this to YHWH's enthronement.
2: "Clouds and thick darkness (ʿānān waʿărāpel) surround him; righteousness and justice (ṣedeq ûmišpāṭ) are the foundation of his throne" — two paired images: the theophanic hiddenness and the moral foundation. Clouds and darkness are the standard Sinai-theophany vocabulary (Exod 19:9, 16; 20:21; Deut 4:11; 5:22-23; 1 Kgs 8:12; Ezek 34:12; Joel 2:2; Zeph 1:15). The hidden, unapproachable divine majesty is surrounded by the same clouds that covered Sinai. "Righteousness and justice are the foundation (mĕkôn) of his throne" — the throne's stability rests not on raw power but on the moral order that defines divine rule. The same formula appears in Ps 89:14 and is the theological claim that power and justice are not in tension in YHWH's governance — his power is the power of right order.
3: "Fire goes out before him (ʾēš lĕpānāyw tēlēk) and consumes his enemies on every side" — the fire before YHWH is the theophanic presence-fire (Exod 13:21-22; 19:18; 24:17; Deut 4:24: "the LORD your God is a consuming fire"; Heb 12:29). The fire that guided Israel in the wilderness is now the fire of judgment that precedes the divine warrior into battle. "Consumes his enemies on every side" — the encircling judgment that leaves no escape route. The divine warrior imagery here (fire, consuming enemies) is the militarized version of the creation-theophany: the God who spoke creation into existence now advances in judgment as a warrior who burns before him.
4: "His lightnings (bĕrāqāyw) illuminate the world; the earth sees and shakes with fear" — YHWH's lightning as the sign of divine speech and action (Pss 18:14; 77:17-18; 144:6; Job 37:3; 38:35). In the ANE, lightning was the weapon of the storm deity — Baal's lightning is his "seven lightnings" in the Ugaritic texts. Psalm 97 demythologizes Baal's lightning as YHWH's own instrument: what Canaanite religion attributed to the storm god belongs to YHWH. "The earth sees and shakes (watāḥēl hāʾāreṣ)" — the earth's trembling (ḥûl, to tremble with birth-pangs) is both a seismic response and a metaphor of creation in the pangs of transformation before the divine presence.
5: "The mountains melt (neharsû hārîm) like wax before the LORD, before the Lord of all the earth" — the mountain-melting theophany is the most dramatic expression of divine power over the created order (Judg 5:5; Mic 1:4; Isa 64:1-3; Nah 1:5). Mountains were the ANE's symbol of permanence and immovability; to say that they melt before YHWH is to assert that the most permanent features of creation are subject to divine will. "Like wax" — the specificity of the wax comparison (wax melts precisely at the point of heat application, flowing away completely) emphasizes not just the power of divine presence but the completeness of the mountain's dissolution. "Lord of all the earth (ʾădōn kol-hāʾāreṣ)" — the title "Lord of the whole earth" appears in the Joshua conquest narratives (Josh 3:11, 13) in connection with the ark leading Israel across the Jordan.
6: "The heavens proclaim (higgîdû šāmayim) his righteousness, and all the peoples behold his glory" — the heavens as witnesses and proclaimers of divine character (also Ps 19:1: "the heavens declare the glory of God"). Nāgad (to declare, to announce, to make known) in the hiphil — the heavens actively declare what they embody and witness. "All the peoples behold his glory (kābôd)" — the universal witness of the divine glory; the creation-proclamation reaches all peoples, establishing a universal accountability. Paul draws on this in Rom 1:18-20: the divine glory is visible in creation to all, leaving all without excuse.
7: "All who worship carved images are put to shame (yēbōšû) — those who boast in worthless idols. All gods bow down before him (hištaḥăwû-lô kol-ʾĕlōhîm)" — the idol-worshipper's shame is the automatic consequence of the theophanic revelation: when the true God appears, all alternatives are exposed as worthless. The bowing of "all gods" — the divine council prostrating before YHWH — is the supreme expression of divine incomparability. Hebrews 1:6 cites this verse: "when he brings the firstborn into the world, he says, 'Let all God's angels worship him'" — applying the prostration of the divine beings to the worship of Christ at the incarnation. The LXX reads ʾĕlōhîm as "angels," and Heb 1:6 follows this LXX tradition to argue for Christ's superiority to the angels.
8: "Zion heard and was glad (šāmĕʿāh watiśmaḥ ṣiyyôn), and the towns of Judah rejoiced because of your judgments, O LORD" — the specific covenant community's response to the theophanic proclamation of vv. 1-7. While "all the earth" (v1) is commanded to rejoice and the nations behold the glory (v6), Zion and the daughters of Judah have a particular, covenant-informed joy in divine judgment. For the nations, divine judgment is threatening; for Zion, which has experienced oppression at the nations' hands, divine judgment against the wicked is liberation. "The towns of Judah (bĕnôt yĕhûdāh)" — literally "daughters of Judah," a personification of the surrounding cities as daughters of the mother-city Jerusalem.
9: "For you, LORD, are most high (ʿelyôn) over all the earth; you are raised far above all gods" — the incomparability statement that grounds the previous verses. ʿElyôn (Most High) is one of the oldest divine titles, possibly pre-Israelite (Gen 14:18-22; Num 24:16; Deut 32:8; Ps 7:17), used for the supreme deity of the Canaanite pantheon before being adopted as a title for YHWH. By using ʿElyôn, the psalm asserts that what the Canaanites attributed to their supreme deity rightly belongs to YHWH. "Far above all gods (meʾōd naʿălêṯā ʿal-kol-ʾĕlōhîm)" — the intensifier meʾōd (very, exceedingly) with the ascension verb: YHWH is not marginally superior but incomparably elevated above all other divine beings.
10: "You who love the LORD, hate evil (śĭnʾû rāʿ)! He guards the lives of his faithful (ḥăsîdāyw) and rescues them from the power of the wicked" — the ethical demand that follows from the theological proclamation. YHWH's kingship is not merely cosmic spectacle but moral demand: the subjects of the divine king must share the king's hatred of what the king hates. "Love" and "hate" in the OT covenantal language are not primarily emotional states but covenant commitments — to love YHWH is to be in covenant relationship; to hate evil is to share YHWH's moral orientation (Prov 8:13: "the fear of the LORD is to hate evil"). "His faithful (ḥăsîdîm)" — those who embody covenant loyalty, the community that reciprocates YHWH's ḥesed.
11: "Light (ʾôr) is sown (zāruaʿ) for the righteous and gladness for the upright in heart" — a striking agricultural metaphor: light is planted like seed, destined to spring up in due season. The image addresses the apparent contradiction of Ps 97: if YHWH reigns in fire and lightning (vv. 3-4), what becomes of the righteous who live in a dark world? The answer: light is planted for them — it is a seed that has already been sown, and its harvest is certain. The eschatological dimension: the present experience of darkness for the righteous is not the final word; the light that has been sown will produce a harvest. Second Temple Judaism developed this into the doctrine of eschatological reward.
12: "Rejoice in the LORD, you who are righteous, and give thanks to his holy name (qodšô)!" — the closing double imperative summarizes the psalm's movement: from theophanic vision (vv. 1-6) to polemic against idols (v7) to Zion's covenant joy (v8) to ethical demand (v10) to eschatological assurance (v11) to the concluding call to rejoice. The righteous are called to rejoice "in the LORD" (bYHWH) — not in their righteousness, not in their circumstances, but in the character and governance of YHWH himself. "His holy name" — the name that encapsulates his character and all his acts; thanksgiving to the name is thanksgiving to the person the name represents.
